Authors: Anatol Garioud; Sébastien Giordano; Nicolas David; Nicolas Gonthier

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.017

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

The growing availability of high-quality Earth observation (EO) data enables accurate global land cover and crop type monitoring. However, the volume and heterogeneity of these datasets pose major processing and annotation challenges. To address this, the French National Institute of Geographical and Forest Information (IGN) is actively exploring how to exploit diverse EO data, which require large annotated datasets. IGN introduces FLAIR-HUB, a novel large-scale multi-sensor land cover dataset with very-high-resolution (20cm) annotations, covering 2528km 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 of France. This dataset uniquely integrates six complementary and aligned modalities, enabling more accurate and fine-grained land cover description: aerial imagery, optical multi-spectral Sentinel-2 and SAR Sentinel-1 satellite time series, high-resolution SPOT satellite images, topographic data, and historical aerial images. Extensive benchmarks evaluate multimodal fusion and deep learning models (convolutional neural networks, transformers) for land cover or crop mapping and also explore multi-task learning. Results underscore the complexity of multimodal fusion and fine-grained classification, with best land cover performance (78.2% accuracy, 65.8% mean Intersection over Union) achieved using nearly all modalities. FLAIR-HUB provides a valuable foundation for advancing supervised and pretraining approaches in Earth Observation research, with data and code openly available.

item.title: SMSDNet: SAR Multi-Scale Dual-Domain Despeckling Network with Feature-Space Diffusion Refinement
item.dc:creator: Tanveer Fatema Khan
Ramesh Kumar Thakur
Mansi Gyanchandani
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: The visual quality and interpretability of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images are significantly degraded by multiplicative speckle noise.This letter proposes SMSDNet, a multi-scale dual-domain deep learning framework for SAR image despeckling that jointly exploits Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) representations to capture complementary spectral characteristics. Unlike existing frequency-domain fusion methods, the proposed approach explicitly leverages phase-sensitive global frequency modeling (FFT) and energy-compact structural representation (DCT) through parallel multi-scale analysis modules, namely Multi-Scale Fourier Analysis (MSFA) and Multi-Scale Cosine Analysis (MSCA).The extracted dual-domain features are fused via residual learning to enhance both speckle suppression and structural preservation. Furthermore, a lightweight feature-space diffusion refinement module, formulated as an iterative residual denoising process with stochastic perturbation, is introduced to progressively suppress residual speckle while preserving fine details.Experimental results on simulated and real SAR datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art techniques in terms of both quantitative metrics and visual quality, while maintaining computational efficiency. Published: 2026-04-27T19:52:49+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tanveer Fatema Khan; Ramesh Kumar Thakur; Mansi Gyanchandani

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3687580

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

The visual quality and interpretability of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images are significantly degraded by multiplicative speckle noise.This letter proposes SMSDNet, a multi-scale dual-domain deep learning framework for SAR image despeckling that jointly exploits Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) representations to capture complementary spectral characteristics. Unlike existing frequency-domain fusion methods, the proposed approach explicitly leverages phase-sensitive global frequency modeling (FFT) and energy-compact structural representation (DCT) through parallel multi-scale analysis modules, namely Multi-Scale Fourier Analysis (MSFA) and Multi-Scale Cosine Analysis (MSCA).The extracted dual-domain features are fused via residual learning to enhance both speckle suppression and structural preservation. Furthermore, a lightweight feature-space diffusion refinement module, formulated as an iterative residual denoising process with stochastic perturbation, is introduced to progressively suppress residual speckle while preserving fine details.Experimental results on simulated and real SAR datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art techniques in terms of both quantitative metrics and visual quality, while maintaining computational efficiency.

item.title: Rethinking the Importance of High-frequency Components in Transformers for Image Restoration
item.dc:creator: Shihao Zhou
Jinshan Pan
Duosheng Chen
Yaopeng Dong
Jufeng Yang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Transformer-based approaches have achieved superior performance in image restoration, since they can model long-term dependencies well. However, the limitation in capturing local information restricts their capacity to remove degradations. While existing approaches attempt to mitigate this issue by incorporating convolutional operations, the core component in Transformer, i.e., self-attention, which serves as a low-pass filter, could unintentionally dilute or even eliminate the acquired local patterns. In this paper, we propose HIT, a simple yet effective High-frequency Injected Transformer for image restoration. Specifically, we design a window-wise injection module (WIM), which incorporates abundant high-frequency details into the feature map, to provide reliable references for restoring high-quality images. We also develop a bidirectional interaction module (BIM) to aggregate features at different scales using a mutually reinforced paradigm, resulting in spatially and contextually improved representations. In addition, we introduce a spatial enhancement unit (SEU) to preserve essential spatial relationships that may be lost due to the computations carried out across channel dimensions in the BIM. Extensive experiments on 6 tasks (real noise, rain streak, blur, flare, underwater conditions, and low-light conditions) demonstrate that HIT with linear computational complexity performs favorably against the state-of-the-art methods. The code will be available upon acceptance. Published: 2026-04-27T19:52:08+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.796 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shihao Zhou; Jinshan Pan; Duosheng Chen; Yaopeng Dong; Jufeng Yang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3685836

Relevance: 0.796 (must_read)

Transformer-based approaches have achieved superior performance in image restoration, since they can model long-term dependencies well. However, the limitation in capturing local information restricts their capacity to remove degradations. While existing approaches attempt to mitigate this issue by incorporating convolutional operations, the core component in Transformer, i.e., self-attention, which serves as a low-pass filter, could unintentionally dilute or even eliminate the acquired local patterns. In this paper, we propose HIT, a simple yet effective High-frequency Injected Transformer for image restoration. Specifically, we design a window-wise injection module (WIM), which incorporates abundant high-frequency details into the feature map, to provide reliable references for restoring high-quality images. We also develop a bidirectional interaction module (BIM) to aggregate features at different scales using a mutually reinforced paradigm, resulting in spatially and contextually improved representations. In addition, we introduce a spatial enhancement unit (SEU) to preserve essential spatial relationships that may be lost due to the computations carried out across channel dimensions in the BIM. Extensive experiments on 6 tasks (real noise, rain streak, blur, flare, underwater conditions, and low-light conditions) demonstrate that HIT with linear computational complexity performs favorably against the state-of-the-art methods. The code will be available upon acceptance.

item.title: PISA-Net: Progressive integrated spatio-spectral attention network guided by physical priors for underwater acoustic target recognition
item.dc:creator: Rongyao Zhao
Jing Xu
Lyufang Zhao
Kun Yu
Jian Hu
Jiaru Chen
Feng Liu
Tongsheng Shen
item.prism:publicationName: Expert Systems with Applications
item.description: Underwater acoustic target recognition (UATR) is a critical technology for various maritime applications. The task is challenged by the complex and non-stationary marine acoustic environment, which often limits the generalization capability of deep learning models due to a lack of explicit constraints on their feature representations. To address this, we propose the progressive integrated spatio-spectral attention network (PISA-Net), an architecture featuring a progressive refinement backbone (PRB) that emulates a coarse-to-fine analytical process. A central component is the integrated spatio-spectral attention (ISSA) module, which is designed to enable a separated analysis of key feature properties. Specifically, ISSA utilizes two parallel branches: the dual-focus sparse attention (DFSA) to analyze local topological structure, and the multi-band adaptive filtering attention (MAFA) to capture periodic properties via the Fourier transform. Furthermore, we introduce a physics-informed frequency regularizer (PIFR), which serves as an inductive bias by imposing a feature smoothness prior on the network. Experiments on the public ShipsEar and DeepShip datasets show that PISA-Net achieves highly competitive performance. Ablation studies systematically validate the efficacy of each proposed component, demonstrating the benefits of the progressive refinement strategy, the integrated spatio-spectral analysis, and the physics-informed regularization. Published: 2026-04-28T07:03:35+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Rongyao Zhao; Jing Xu; Lyufang Zhao; Kun Yu; Jian Hu; Jiaru Chen; Feng Liu; Tongsheng Shen

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132595

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Underwater acoustic target recognition (UATR) is a critical technology for various maritime applications. The task is challenged by the complex and non-stationary marine acoustic environment, which often limits the generalization capability of deep learning models due to a lack of explicit constraints on their feature representations. To address this, we propose the progressive integrated spatio-spectral attention network (PISA-Net), an architecture featuring a progressive refinement backbone (PRB) that emulates a coarse-to-fine analytical process. A central component is the integrated spatio-spectral attention (ISSA) module, which is designed to enable a separated analysis of key feature properties. Specifically, ISSA utilizes two parallel branches: the dual-focus sparse attention (DFSA) to analyze local topological structure, and the multi-band adaptive filtering attention (MAFA) to capture periodic properties via the Fourier transform. Furthermore, we introduce a physics-informed frequency regularizer (PIFR), which serves as an inductive bias by imposing a feature smoothness prior on the network. Experiments on the public ShipsEar and DeepShip datasets show that PISA-Net achieves highly competitive performance. Ablation studies systematically validate the efficacy of each proposed component, demonstrating the benefits of the progressive refinement strategy, the integrated spatio-spectral analysis, and the physics-informed regularization.

item.title: Multi-scale and Multi-level Feature Fusion for Small Object Detection in Sonar Images
item.dc:creator: Xu Gao
Cuicui Zhang
Xiaobo Li
Bin Xue
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Target detection in sonar images holds significant implications for ecological conservation, marine engineering, and military applications, etc. However, existing object detection models which are mostly developed based on fixed receptive fields are challenged by detecting multi-scale objects, especially detecting small-scale objects from low-quality sonar images. To address this issue, we propose a novel multi-scale and multi-level feature fusion framework built upon You Only Look Once (YOLO), namely YOLO11s-MSML for object detection in sonar images. This architecture performs feature fusion from varying receptive fields within the same layer while enabling efficient cross-layer fusion of shallow and deep features, thereby strengthening detection capabilities across scales, particularly for small objects. Multi-scale spatial features are extracted using different-sized convolutional kernels via a Receptive Field Block (RFB) module within the same layer; multi-level features from cross-layers are integrated through a novel vertical module combining Content-Guided Attention (CGA) and SimAM attention with slicing operation (SWS). In addition, for model lightweight design, Distribution Shifting Convolution (DSC) is introduced to improve the model efficiency. Experimental results on the public Sonar Common Target Detection dataset (SCTD) demonstrate our model's superiority over existing algorithms, achieving 96.6%mAP0.5 for all objects. Compared with the state-of-the-art method, our algorithm improved detection accuracy of small objects by 3.3%. Published: 2026-04-27T19:52:49+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xu Gao; Cuicui Zhang; Xiaobo Li; Bin Xue

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3687803

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Target detection in sonar images holds significant implications for ecological conservation, marine engineering, and military applications, etc. However, existing object detection models which are mostly developed based on fixed receptive fields are challenged by detecting multi-scale objects, especially detecting small-scale objects from low-quality sonar images. To address this issue, we propose a novel multi-scale and multi-level feature fusion framework built upon You Only Look Once (YOLO), namely YOLO11s-MSML for object detection in sonar images. This architecture performs feature fusion from varying receptive fields within the same layer while enabling efficient cross-layer fusion of shallow and deep features, thereby strengthening detection capabilities across scales, particularly for small objects. Multi-scale spatial features are extracted using different-sized convolutional kernels via a Receptive Field Block (RFB) module within the same layer; multi-level features from cross-layers are integrated through a novel vertical module combining Content-Guided Attention (CGA) and SimAM attention with slicing operation (SWS). In addition, for model lightweight design, Distribution Shifting Convolution (DSC) is introduced to improve the model efficiency. Experimental results on the public Sonar Common Target Detection dataset (SCTD) demonstrate our model's superiority over existing algorithms, achieving 96.6%mAP0.5 for all objects. Compared with the state-of-the-art method, our algorithm improved detection accuracy of small objects by 3.3%.

item.title: UKT-GAN：面向SAR到光学图像双保真转换的单向知识迁移生成对抗网络
item.dc:creator: Zhang Zhihao
Fu Zhitao
Ji Yashuai
Zhang Xinshan
Tang Bohui
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Image and Graphics
item.description: 目的针对现有SAR到光学图像转换方法采用单一生成器难以兼顾全局语义一致性与局部纹理真实性的问题，本文提出一种单向知识迁移生成对抗网络（unidirectional knowledge transfer generative adversarial network，UKT-GAN），通过双分支网络间的单向知识迁移，实现生成图像在全局与局部层面的双重保真。方法UKT-GAN由细节重建子网络与语义保持子网络构成。其中，细节重建子网络专注于图像局部纹理结构细节的生成，语义保持子网络则负责保障全局语义信息的一致性。通过在两个子网络的输出中添加单向一致性约束，将细节重建子网络的细节生成能力迁移至语义保持子网络，来对语义保持子网络的生成图像进行局部纹理结构细节的优化调整。从而确保语义保持子网络生成的图像在全局语义信息与局部纹理结构细节上均具备高度的保真度。结果在本文的5个数据集上，相比于当前主流的6种转换方法，UKT-GAN取得了更优的定量结果。在SEN1-2的农田、山地子数据集和WHU-OPT-SAR数据集上，其PSNR、SSIM、LPIPS和RMSE四项指标全面领先；在SEN1-2的建筑物和森林子数据集上，其SSIM和LPIPS两项指标取得了最优值。结论实验结果表明，在SAR到光学图像转换方面，UKT-GAN通过单向知识迁移能够有效整合细节重建子网络和语义保持子网络间的互补性优势，生成结构更清晰、细节更丰富的光学图像。本文代码开源地址：https：//www.scidb.cn/s/YNjqIf。 Published: 2026-04-27T07:36:08+00:00 Venue: Journal of Image and Graphics Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhang Zhihao; Fu Zhitao; Ji Yashuai; Zhang Xinshan; Tang Bohui

Journal: Journal of Image and Graphics

DOI: 10.11834/jig.250573

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

目的针对现有SAR到光学图像转换方法采用单一生成器难以兼顾全局语义一致性与局部纹理真实性的问题，本文提出一种单向知识迁移生成对抗网络（unidirectional knowledge transfer generative adversarial network，UKT-GAN），通过双分支网络间的单向知识迁移，实现生成图像在全局与局部层面的双重保真。方法UKT-GAN由细节重建子网络与语义保持子网络构成。其中，细节重建子网络专注于图像局部纹理结构细节的生成，语义保持子网络则负责保障全局语义信息的一致性。通过在两个子网络的输出中添加单向一致性约束，将细节重建子网络的细节生成能力迁移至语义保持子网络，来对语义保持子网络的生成图像进行局部纹理结构细节的优化调整。从而确保语义保持子网络生成的图像在全局语义信息与局部纹理结构细节上均具备高度的保真度。结果在本文的5个数据集上，相比于当前主流的6种转换方法，UKT-GAN取得了更优的定量结果。在SEN1-2的农田、山地子数据集和WHU-OPT-SAR数据集上，其PSNR、SSIM、LPIPS和RMSE四项指标全面领先；在SEN1-2的建筑物和森林子数据集上，其SSIM和LPIPS两项指标取得了最优值。结论实验结果表明，在SAR到光学图像转换方面，UKT-GAN通过单向知识迁移能够有效整合细节重建子网络和语义保持子网络间的互补性优势，生成结构更清晰、细节更丰富的光学图像。本文代码开源地址：https：//www.scidb.cn/s/YNjqIf。

item.title: Training-Free VFM-guided Dynamic Refinement for Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Yuhang Zhang
Binbin Wei
Wei Li
Xin Yang
Tiantian Zeng
Wenbin Zou
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation (DGSS) has recently attracted lots of research attention aiming to achieve robust segmentation performance on unseen domains, which aligns with diverse real-world applications. Visual Foundation Model (VFM), depending on large-scale pre-trained data and exquisite training strategies with good generalizability, has been explored and mined in some downstream tasks like DGSS. However, existing VFM-based DGSS methods predominantly focus on fine-tuning the VFM to adapt them to the semantic segmentation task. While this improves task alignment, it inevitably introduces additional training overhead and still results in static prediction behavior at inference time, making the model unable to adapt to varying target-domain distributions during deployment. To address these issues, we propose a Training-free VFM-guided Dynamic Refinement (TVDR) framework for the DGSS task, operating at the inference stage. First, a maximum category voting module is proposed to smooth the segmentation result of large masks generated by Segment Anything Model (SAM). Second, a small-object under-segmentation optimization strategy is proposed to improve the generalization of the small objects. Finally, a fusion refinement module is designed to refine the segmentation results of the above strategies. Compared with existing methods, our strategy does not require any parameter updates for VFM and has the advantages of plug-and-play and flexible deployment. It provides an efficient and practical new paradigm for cross-domain semantic segmentation tasks. Extensive experiments on widely-used benchmarks verified the effectiveness of the proposed approaches. Code is available at: https://github.com/Hectoor/TVDR. Published: 2026-04-27T19:51:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuhang Zhang; Binbin Wei; Wei Li; Xin Yang; Tiantian Zeng; Wenbin Zou

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3687250

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation (DGSS) has recently attracted lots of research attention aiming to achieve robust segmentation performance on unseen domains, which aligns with diverse real-world applications. Visual Foundation Model (VFM), depending on large-scale pre-trained data and exquisite training strategies with good generalizability, has been explored and mined in some downstream tasks like DGSS. However, existing VFM-based DGSS methods predominantly focus on fine-tuning the VFM to adapt them to the semantic segmentation task. While this improves task alignment, it inevitably introduces additional training overhead and still results in static prediction behavior at inference time, making the model unable to adapt to varying target-domain distributions during deployment. To address these issues, we propose a Training-free VFM-guided Dynamic Refinement (TVDR) framework for the DGSS task, operating at the inference stage. First, a maximum category voting module is proposed to smooth the segmentation result of large masks generated by Segment Anything Model (SAM). Second, a small-object under-segmentation optimization strategy is proposed to improve the generalization of the small objects. Finally, a fusion refinement module is designed to refine the segmentation results of the above strategies. Compared with existing methods, our strategy does not require any parameter updates for VFM and has the advantages of plug-and-play and flexible deployment. It provides an efficient and practical new paradigm for cross-domain semantic segmentation tasks. Extensive experiments on widely-used benchmarks verified the effectiveness of the proposed approaches. Code is available at: https://github.com/Hectoor/TVDR.

item.title: CLLAP: Contrastive Learning-based LiDAR-Augmented Pretraining for Enhanced Radar-Camera Fusion
item.dc:creator: Bingyi Liu
Chuanhui Zhu
Hongfei Xue
Jian Teng
Jipeng Liu
Enshu Wang
Penglin Dai
Pu Wang
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Accurate 3D object detection is critical for autonomous driving, necessitating reliable, cost-effective sensors capable of operating in adverse weather conditions. Camera and millimeter-wave radar fusion has emerged as a promising solution; however, these methods often rely on finely annotated radar data, which is scarce and labor-intensive to produce. To address this challenge, we present CLLAP, a Contrastive Learning-based LiDAR-Augmented Pretraining framework that enhances the performance of existing radar-camera fusion methods for 3D object detection. CLLAP leverages abundant LiDAR data to generate pseudo-radar data using the proposed L2R (LiDAR-to-Radar) Sampling method. Then, it incorporates this data into a novel dual-stage, dual-modality contrastive learning strategy, enabling effective self-supervised learning from paired pseudo-radar and image data. This approach facilitates effective pretraining of existing radar-camera fusion models in a plug-and-play manner, enhancing their feature extraction capabilities and improving detection accuracy and robustness. Experimental results using NuScenes and Lyft Level 5 datasets demonstrate significant performance improvements across three baseline models, highlighting CLLAP's effectiveness in advancing radar-camera fusion for autonomous driving applications. Published: 2026-04-27T05:07:45+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Bingyi Liu; Chuanhui Zhu; Hongfei Xue; Jian Teng; Jipeng Liu; Enshu Wang; Penglin Dai; Pu Wang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Accurate 3D object detection is critical for autonomous driving, necessitating reliable, cost-effective sensors capable of operating in adverse weather conditions. Camera and millimeter-wave radar fusion has emerged as a promising solution; however, these methods often rely on finely annotated radar data, which is scarce and labor-intensive to produce. To address this challenge, we present CLLAP, a Contrastive Learning-based LiDAR-Augmented Pretraining framework that enhances the performance of existing radar-camera fusion methods for 3D object detection. CLLAP leverages abundant LiDAR data to generate pseudo-radar data using the proposed L2R (LiDAR-to-Radar) Sampling method. Then, it incorporates this data into a novel dual-stage, dual-modality contrastive learning strategy, enabling effective self-supervised learning from paired pseudo-radar and image data. This approach facilitates effective pretraining of existing radar-camera fusion models in a plug-and-play manner, enhancing their feature extraction capabilities and improving detection accuracy and robustness. Experimental results using NuScenes and Lyft Level 5 datasets demonstrate significant performance improvements across three baseline models, highlighting CLLAP's effectiveness in advancing radar-camera fusion for autonomous driving applications.

item.title: PMPSR: Towards Real-World Image Super-Resolution with Pre-trained Multimodal Large Language Model Priors
item.dc:creator: Detian Huang
Miaohua Ruan
Luanyuan Dai
Yaohui Guo
Zhenzhen Hu
Huanqiang Zeng
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
item.description: Pre-trained Text-to-Image (T2I) diffusion models, attributed to their powerful generative priors, have shown exceptional performance in real-world image super-resolution tasks. However, when dealing with severely degraded images, these models tend to reconstruct the images with semantic inaccuracies due to disrupted local structures and produce inconsistent content or unnatural visual artifacts caused by the complex and diverse nature of real-world degradations. To address these challenges, we propose PMPSR, a novel real-world image Super-Resolution network based on Pre-trained Multimodal large language model Priors. By incorporating these priors, PMPSR reconstructs realistic images while preserving semantic fidelity. Specifically, we first design a specialized instruction strategy to effectively interface with the pre-trained multimodal large language model. This strategy enables the extraction of high-level semantic priors aligned with human visual perception while identifying accurate degradation priors from the images to be reconstructed. Then, we propose an adaptive Prompt Attention (PA) module to balance different types of priors. Finally, we develop a simple yet effective Texture Restoration Cross-Attention (TRCA) module to strengthen fine-grained texture reconstruction. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed PMPSR achieves state-of-the-art performance in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations. Published: 2026-04-27T19:50:24+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Detian Huang; Miaohua Ruan; Luanyuan Dai; Yaohui Guo; Zhenzhen Hu; Huanqiang Zeng

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3687005

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Pre-trained Text-to-Image (T2I) diffusion models, attributed to their powerful generative priors, have shown exceptional performance in real-world image super-resolution tasks. However, when dealing with severely degraded images, these models tend to reconstruct the images with semantic inaccuracies due to disrupted local structures and produce inconsistent content or unnatural visual artifacts caused by the complex and diverse nature of real-world degradations. To address these challenges, we propose PMPSR, a novel real-world image Super-Resolution network based on Pre-trained Multimodal large language model Priors. By incorporating these priors, PMPSR reconstructs realistic images while preserving semantic fidelity. Specifically, we first design a specialized instruction strategy to effectively interface with the pre-trained multimodal large language model. This strategy enables the extraction of high-level semantic priors aligned with human visual perception while identifying accurate degradation priors from the images to be reconstructed. Then, we propose an adaptive Prompt Attention (PA) module to balance different types of priors. Finally, we develop a simple yet effective Texture Restoration Cross-Attention (TRCA) module to strengthen fine-grained texture reconstruction. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed PMPSR achieves state-of-the-art performance in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations.

item.title: MFR-Net: Motion-Guided Feature Refinement Network for Video Small Object Detection in Vision-Based Airport Surveillance Systems
item.dc:creator: Shengjie Zhang
Yang Yang
Yanbo Zhu
Shengsheng Qian
Xiaoxiao Zhang
Kaiquan Cai
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Video-based surveillance in critical airport surface environments is essential for comprehensive situational awareness, yet it presents a unique and challenging task which we formally define as Video Small Object Detection in Vision-based Airport Surveillance Systems (VSOD-VASS). This task is characterized by a confluence of compounding difficulties, including 1) diminutive object size and severe long-tail imbalance of semantic categories, and 2) consistent modeling of long-range motions and stochastic transient dynamics. To address these challenges, we propose a two-part framework: an offline data augmentation pipeline, named Trajectory-anchored Object Augmentation (TOA), and a Motion-guided Feature Refinement Network (MFR-Net). The TOA pipeline combines trajectory-constrained object generation for controlled appearance diversification and delayed trajectory replay for reusing valid historical object states. The MFR-Net then integrates two key innovations: an adaptive Motion-aware Feature Alignment (MFA) module that robustly models transient dynamics from adjacent frames, and a selective Distant Proximal Temporal Feature Aggregation (DPTFA) module that uses attention to filter noise and aggregate valuable long-range dependencies. To facilitate robust evaluation, we introduce VASSO, a large-scale real-world airport surveillance dataset featuring 1,332,223 instances across 12 categories, where over 87.51% of all objects are small or tiny. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MFR-Net achieves performance with 72.50% m A P mAP and 93.43% m A P 50 mAP_{50} , significantly improving 4.70% m A P mAP with existing SOTA methods. Our work provides a formal problem definition, a strong SOTA method, and a challenging large-scale dataset to advance research in this critical domain. Code is available at https://github.com/ATMHub-515/MFR-Net Published: 2026-04-27T19:51:15+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.790 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shengjie Zhang; Yang Yang; Yanbo Zhu; Shengsheng Qian; Xiaoxiao Zhang; Kaiquan Cai

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tits.2026.3686215

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Video-based surveillance in critical airport surface environments is essential for comprehensive situational awareness, yet it presents a unique and challenging task which we formally define as Video Small Object Detection in Vision-based Airport Surveillance Systems (VSOD-VASS). This task is characterized by a confluence of compounding difficulties, including 1) diminutive object size and severe long-tail imbalance of semantic categories, and 2) consistent modeling of long-range motions and stochastic transient dynamics. To address these challenges, we propose a two-part framework: an offline data augmentation pipeline, named Trajectory-anchored Object Augmentation (TOA), and a Motion-guided Feature Refinement Network (MFR-Net). The TOA pipeline combines trajectory-constrained object generation for controlled appearance diversification and delayed trajectory replay for reusing valid historical object states. The MFR-Net then integrates two key innovations: an adaptive Motion-aware Feature Alignment (MFA) module that robustly models transient dynamics from adjacent frames, and a selective Distant Proximal Temporal Feature Aggregation (DPTFA) module that uses attention to filter noise and aggregate valuable long-range dependencies. To facilitate robust evaluation, we introduce VASSO, a large-scale real-world airport surveillance dataset featuring 1,332,223 instances across 12 categories, where over 87.51% of all objects are small or tiny. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MFR-Net achieves performance with 72.50% m A P mAP and 93.43% m A P 50 mAP_{50} , significantly improving 4.70% m A P mAP with existing SOTA methods. Our work provides a formal problem definition, a strong SOTA method, and a challenging large-scale dataset to advance research in this critical domain. Code is available at https://github.com/ATMHub-515/MFR-Net

item.title: DAFSDet: Dual-Attention Guided Few-Shot Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Guangshuai Gao
Zhilin Zhang
Wei Zhang
Yunqi Shang
Yan Dong
Jiangtao Xi
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: Few-shot object detection aims to accurately identify and localize novel categories using only a small number of labeled samples. In remote sensing images, however, this task faces significant challenges due to substantial variations in target scale and complex backgrounds. To address these issues, this paper proposes a dual-attention guided few-shot object detection framework, DAFSDet. Specifically, a dual-attention strategy is implemented across the feature modeling and proposal generation stages. For feature fusion, the Content-Aware Strip Pyramid (CASP) is designed to enhance multi-scale feature representation by modeling spatial and contextual information. In the detection stage, a Deformable Attention RPN (DA-RPN) is proposed to improve the localization quality of candidate regions. With these designs, the proposed method effectively mitigates the challenges posed by multi-scale variations and complex backgrounds. Experimental results on the DIOR and NWPU VHR-10 datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over baseline methods, with notable gains of 7.54 mAP on DIOR Split 2 under the 10-shot setting and 2.09 mAP on NWPU VHR-10 under the 3-shot setting. These results indicate that the proposed method offers an effective solution for few-shot object detection in complex remote sensing scenarios. Published: 2026-04-28T07:49:36+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guangshuai Gao; Zhilin Zhang; Wei Zhang; Yunqi Shang; Yan Dong; Jiangtao Xi

Journal: Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.3390/rs18091345

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

Few-shot object detection aims to accurately identify and localize novel categories using only a small number of labeled samples. In remote sensing images, however, this task faces significant challenges due to substantial variations in target scale and complex backgrounds. To address these issues, this paper proposes a dual-attention guided few-shot object detection framework, DAFSDet. Specifically, a dual-attention strategy is implemented across the feature modeling and proposal generation stages. For feature fusion, the Content-Aware Strip Pyramid (CASP) is designed to enhance multi-scale feature representation by modeling spatial and contextual information. In the detection stage, a Deformable Attention RPN (DA-RPN) is proposed to improve the localization quality of candidate regions. With these designs, the proposed method effectively mitigates the challenges posed by multi-scale variations and complex backgrounds. Experimental results on the DIOR and NWPU VHR-10 datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over baseline methods, with notable gains of 7.54 mAP on DIOR Split 2 under the 10-shot setting and 2.09 mAP on NWPU VHR-10 under the 3-shot setting. These results indicate that the proposed method offers an effective solution for few-shot object detection in complex remote sensing scenarios.

item.title: A General Representation-Based Approach to Multi-Source Domain Adaptation
item.dc:creator: Ignavier Ng
Yan Li
Zijian Li
Yujia Zheng
Guangyi Chen
Kun Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: A central problem in unsupervised domain adaptation is determining what to transfer from labeled source domains to an unlabeled target domain. To handle high-dimensional observations (e.g., images), a line of approaches use deep learning to learn latent representations of the observations, which facilitate knowledge transfer in the latent space. However, existing approaches often rely on restrictive assumptions to establish identifiability of the joint distribution in the target domain, such as independent latent variables or invariant label distributions, limiting their real-world applicability. In this work, we propose a general domain adaptation framework that learns compact latent representations to capture distribution shifts relative to the prediction task and address the fundamental question of what representations should be learned and transferred. Notably, we first demonstrate that learning representations based on all the predictive information, i.e., the label's Markov blanket in terms of the learned representations, is often underspecified in general settings. Instead, we show that, interestingly, general domain adaptation can be achieved by partitioning the representations of Markov blanket into those of the label's parents, children, and spouses. Moreover, its identifiability guarantee can be established. Building on these theoretical insights, we develop a practical, nonparametric approach for domain adaptation in a general setting, which can handle different types of distribution shifts. Published: 2026-04-26T16:29:32+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.786 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ignavier Ng; Yan Li; Zijian Li; Yujia Zheng; Guangyi Chen; Kun Zhang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.786 (must_read)

A central problem in unsupervised domain adaptation is determining what to transfer from labeled source domains to an unlabeled target domain. To handle high-dimensional observations (e.g., images), a line of approaches use deep learning to learn latent representations of the observations, which facilitate knowledge transfer in the latent space. However, existing approaches often rely on restrictive assumptions to establish identifiability of the joint distribution in the target domain, such as independent latent variables or invariant label distributions, limiting their real-world applicability. In this work, we propose a general domain adaptation framework that learns compact latent representations to capture distribution shifts relative to the prediction task and address the fundamental question of what representations should be learned and transferred. Notably, we first demonstrate that learning representations based on all the predictive information, i.e., the label's Markov blanket in terms of the learned representations, is often underspecified in general settings. Instead, we show that, interestingly, general domain adaptation can be achieved by partitioning the representations of Markov blanket into those of the label's parents, children, and spouses. Moreover, its identifiability guarantee can be established. Building on these theoretical insights, we develop a practical, nonparametric approach for domain adaptation in a general setting, which can handle different types of distribution shifts.

item.title: RDCNet: Rotation-aware Feature Representation and Aspect-Adaptive Label Assignment for Efficient Arbitrary-Oriented Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Jihye Ryu
Jaegeun Yoon
Kwangho Song
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Recent advances in Arbitrary-Oriented Object Detection (AOOD) for remote sensing imagery have largely relied on high-capacity architectures, such as two-stage detectors and transformer-based models, to handle arbitrary object orientations and extreme aspect ratios. Although these approaches achieve high detection accuracy, they often incur substantial computational overhead, limiting their applicability in real time scenarios. In contrast, one-stage detectors offer superior efficiency, but typically suffer from insufficient rotation-aware feature representation, resulting in suboptimal localization accuracy. We introduce Rotation-aware Deformable Convolution Network (RDCNet), an efficient one-stage anchor-free architecture for AOOD. RDCNet incorporates a rotation-aware deformable convolution module into the backbone, where offset sampling is formulated in polar coordinates to decouple scale and orientation, enabling stable encoding of rotational information at early-stage feature maps. This rotation-aware representation is propagated through deeper layers enriched with semantic information. Furthermore, we introduce an Aspect-Adaptive Label Assignment (AALA) strategy that integrates an aspect-agnostic centerness term to guide positive sample selection, effectively reducing learning bias toward objects with extreme aspect ratios. Extensive experiments on AOOD benchmarks, including DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5, and HRSC2016, demonstrate that RDCNet achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance among one stage detectors while maintaining real-time inference speed, and exhibits favorable efficiency–accuracy trade-offs compared to representative two-stage approaches. These results indicate that RDCNet provides a practical and effective solution for AOOD in complex remote sensing scenes. Published: 2026-04-27T19:49:45+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.785 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jihye Ryu; Jaegeun Yoon; Kwangho Song

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3687230

Relevance: 0.785 (must_read)

Recent advances in Arbitrary-Oriented Object Detection (AOOD) for remote sensing imagery have largely relied on high-capacity architectures, such as two-stage detectors and transformer-based models, to handle arbitrary object orientations and extreme aspect ratios. Although these approaches achieve high detection accuracy, they often incur substantial computational overhead, limiting their applicability in real time scenarios. In contrast, one-stage detectors offer superior efficiency, but typically suffer from insufficient rotation-aware feature representation, resulting in suboptimal localization accuracy. We introduce Rotation-aware Deformable Convolution Network (RDCNet), an efficient one-stage anchor-free architecture for AOOD. RDCNet incorporates a rotation-aware deformable convolution module into the backbone, where offset sampling is formulated in polar coordinates to decouple scale and orientation, enabling stable encoding of rotational information at early-stage feature maps. This rotation-aware representation is propagated through deeper layers enriched with semantic information. Furthermore, we introduce an Aspect-Adaptive Label Assignment (AALA) strategy that integrates an aspect-agnostic centerness term to guide positive sample selection, effectively reducing learning bias toward objects with extreme aspect ratios. Extensive experiments on AOOD benchmarks, including DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5, and HRSC2016, demonstrate that RDCNet achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance among one stage detectors while maintaining real-time inference speed, and exhibits favorable efficiency–accuracy trade-offs compared to representative two-stage approaches. These results indicate that RDCNet provides a practical and effective solution for AOOD in complex remote sensing scenes.

item.title: SDFRF-Net: Spatial Diversity Fine-Grained Representation and Fusion Network
item.dc:creator: Yuelan Feng
Donglin Jing
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: In remote sensing images, due to the significant changes in target scale, arbitrary orientation and high aspect ratio, achieving precise detection of rotating targets still faces huge challenges. Although existing methods have made progress in multi-scale feature fusion or direction-aware design, they usually treat scale and direction as independent problems, ignoring their intrinsic coupling relationship. This fragmented modeling leads to spatial misalignment problems during the detection process, especially for slender targets such as cargo ships and passenger ships, where the accurate estimation of their directions highly depends on the precise extraction of scale features. To solve the above problems, this paper proposes SDFRF-Net. Specifically, we first design the spatial diversity dynamic fusion module FRRD, which adopts stacked hollow small convolution kernels, integrates Ghost linear transformation and spatial attention, and dynamic weighted fusion to extract fine-grained scale features. Secondly, an anchor box is designed to guide the deformable convolutional module GMP. The directional anchor box guides the rotation of the convolutional kernel and equiinterval sampling to adapt to the target shape. Finally, the equilibrium loss Function (LEC) is designed to enhance the model’s attention to small gradient samples by balancing the L1 loss and improve the positioning accuracy. We conducted extensive experiments on the DOTA and HRSC2016 datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of the model we proposed. Published: 2026-04-27T19:52:49+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.784 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuelan Feng; Donglin Jing

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3687351

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

In remote sensing images, due to the significant changes in target scale, arbitrary orientation and high aspect ratio, achieving precise detection of rotating targets still faces huge challenges. Although existing methods have made progress in multi-scale feature fusion or direction-aware design, they usually treat scale and direction as independent problems, ignoring their intrinsic coupling relationship. This fragmented modeling leads to spatial misalignment problems during the detection process, especially for slender targets such as cargo ships and passenger ships, where the accurate estimation of their directions highly depends on the precise extraction of scale features. To solve the above problems, this paper proposes SDFRF-Net. Specifically, we first design the spatial diversity dynamic fusion module FRRD, which adopts stacked hollow small convolution kernels, integrates Ghost linear transformation and spatial attention, and dynamic weighted fusion to extract fine-grained scale features. Secondly, an anchor box is designed to guide the deformable convolutional module GMP. The directional anchor box guides the rotation of the convolutional kernel and equiinterval sampling to adapt to the target shape. Finally, the equilibrium loss Function (LEC) is designed to enhance the model’s attention to small gradient samples by balancing the L1 loss and improve the positioning accuracy. We conducted extensive experiments on the DOTA and HRSC2016 datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of the model we proposed.

item.title: Efficient Real-World Image Super-Resolution Via Adaptive Directional Gradient Convolution
item.dc:creator: Long Peng
Zhanfeng Feng
Renjing Pei
Wenbo Li
Jiaming Guo
Xueyang Fu
Yang Wang
Yang Cao
Zheng-Jun Zha
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Computer Vision
item.description: Real-world image super-resolution (Real-SR) endeavors to produce high-resolution images with rich details while mitigating the impact of multiple degradation factors. Although existing methods have achieved impressive achievements in detail recovery, they still fall short when addressing regions with complex gradient arrangements due to the intensity-based linear weighting feature extraction manner. Moreover, the stochastic artifacts introduced by degradation cues during the imaging process in real LR increase the disorder of the overall image details, further complicating the perception of intrinsic gradient arrangement. To address these challenges, we innovatively introduce kernel-wise differential operations within the convolutional kernel and develop several learnable directional gradient convolutions. These convolutions are integrated in parallel with a novel linear weighting mechanism to form an Adaptive Directional Gradient Convolution (DGConv), which adaptively weights and fuses the basic directional gradients to improve the gradient arrangement perception capability for both regular and irregular textures. Coupled with DGConv, we further devise a novel equivalent parameter fusion method for DGConv that maintains its rich representational capabilities while keeping computational costs consistent with a single Vanilla Convolution (VConv), enabling DGConv to improve the performance of existing super-resolution networks without incurring additional computational expenses. To better leverage the superiority of DGConv, we further develop an Adaptive Information Interaction Block (AIIBlock) to adeptly balance the enhancement of texture and contrast while meticulously investigating the interdependencies, culminating in the creation of a Directional Gradient Perceiving Network (DGPNet) for Real-SR through simple stacking. Comparative results with 15 state-of-the-art (SOTA) super-resolution methods across three public datasets underscore the effectiveness and efficiency of our proposed approach. The source code will be made publicly available at github.com/peylnog/ERealSR-DGPNet . Published: 2026-04-27T18:34:29+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.784 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Long Peng; Zhanfeng Feng; Renjing Pei; Wenbo Li; Jiaming Guo; Xueyang Fu; Yang Wang; Yang Cao; Zheng-Jun Zha

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02843-x

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

Real-world image super-resolution (Real-SR) endeavors to produce high-resolution images with rich details while mitigating the impact of multiple degradation factors. Although existing methods have achieved impressive achievements in detail recovery, they still fall short when addressing regions with complex gradient arrangements due to the intensity-based linear weighting feature extraction manner. Moreover, the stochastic artifacts introduced by degradation cues during the imaging process in real LR increase the disorder of the overall image details, further complicating the perception of intrinsic gradient arrangement. To address these challenges, we innovatively introduce kernel-wise differential operations within the convolutional kernel and develop several learnable directional gradient convolutions. These convolutions are integrated in parallel with a novel linear weighting mechanism to form an Adaptive Directional Gradient Convolution (DGConv), which adaptively weights and fuses the basic directional gradients to improve the gradient arrangement perception capability for both regular and irregular textures. Coupled with DGConv, we further devise a novel equivalent parameter fusion method for DGConv that maintains its rich representational capabilities while keeping computational costs consistent with a single Vanilla Convolution (VConv), enabling DGConv to improve the performance of existing super-resolution networks without incurring additional computational expenses. To better leverage the superiority of DGConv, we further develop an Adaptive Information Interaction Block (AIIBlock) to adeptly balance the enhancement of texture and contrast while meticulously investigating the interdependencies, culminating in the creation of a Directional Gradient Perceiving Network (DGPNet) for Real-SR through simple stacking. Comparative results with 15 state-of-the-art (SOTA) super-resolution methods across three public datasets underscore the effectiveness and efficiency of our proposed approach. The source code will be made publicly available at github.com/peylnog/ERealSR-DGPNet .

item.title: UAGM: Uncertainty-Aware Geometric Modeling for Multi-Scenario 3-D Object Detection in Autonomous Vehicles
item.dc:creator: Zhaojie Sun
Wanru Xu
Lu Shi
Yigang Cen
Yi Jin
Yidong Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Vision-based 3-D object detection is a core task for autonomous driving and intelligent transport systems. However, the idealized geometric assumptions and single-view modeling methods relied upon by existing methods are susceptible to geometric uncertainties, caused by external parameter perturbations and depth estimation noise in practical multi-scenario deployments. Building a unified and robust perception model suitable for multi-scenarios of both ego-vehicle and roadside remains challenging. To address these challenges, we propose an Uncertainty-Aware Geometric Modeling (UAGM) method that explicitly handles geometric uncertainty to achieve robust perception across multiple scenarios. We use a dual-branch architecture to establish a robust geometric foundation: the height branch introduces dynamic virtual coordinate calibration to compensate for camera extrinsic parameter perturbations in real time, while explicitly modeling height prediction uncertainty through Multi-Hypothesis Projection (MHP), thereby constructing a robust global geometric representation. Meanwhile, the depth branch integrates a Probabilistic Depth Smoothing (PDS) module that employs Conditional Random Fields (CRF) to model spatial consistency constraints, effectively mitigating geometric discontinuities arising from pixel-level predictions. To facilitate better information fusion and interaction, we first propose a Temporal Pyramid Fusion (TPF) module to effectively capture multi-scale spatio-temporal dynamics to reduce the uncertainty in single-frame estimation, instead of error-prone dynamic ego-motion compensation. Subsequently, our Hierarchical Refinement Decoder (HRD) refines BEV proposal localization by fusing image features with depth embeddings to compensate for spatial distortions caused by forward projection. Experimental results demonstrate that UAGM not only achieves state-of-the-art detection performance on both the nuScenes and DAIR-V2X benchmarks, but more importantly, it succ... Published: 2026-04-27T19:51:15+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.784 (must_read)

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

However, the idealized geometric assumptions and single-view modeling methods relied upon by existing methods are susceptible to geometric uncertainties, caused by external parameter perturbations and depth estimation noise in practical multi-scenario deployments. Building a unified and robust perception model suitable for multi-scenarios of both ego-vehicle and roadside remains challenging. To address these challenges, we propose an Uncertainty-Aware Geometric Modeling (UAGM) method that explicitly handles geometric uncertainty to achieve robust perception across multiple scenarios. We use a dual-branch architecture to establish a robust geometric foundation: the height branch introduces dynamic virtual coordinate calibration to compensate for camera extrinsic parameter perturbations in real time, while explicitly modeling height prediction uncertainty through Multi-Hypothesis Projection (MHP), thereby constructing a robust global geometric representation. Meanwhile, the depth branch integrates a Probabilistic Depth Smoothing (PDS) module that employs Conditional Random Fields (CRF) to model spatial consistency constraints, effectively mitigating geometric discontinuities arising from pixel-level predictions. To facilitate better information fusion and interaction, we first propose a Temporal Pyramid Fusion (TPF) module to effectively capture multi-scale spatio-temporal dynamics to reduce the uncertainty in single-frame estimation, instead of error-prone dynamic ego-motion compensation. Subsequently, our Hierarchical Refinement Decoder (HRD) refines BEV proposal localization by fusing image features with depth embeddings to compensate for spatial distortions caused by forward projection. Experimental results demonstrate that UAGM not only achieves state-of-the-art detection performance on both the nuScenes and DAIR-V2X benchmarks, but more importantly, it succ...

item.title: PhysSANet: Formation-Inspired Perturbations and Structure-Aware Learning for Semi-Supervised Remote Sensing Change Detection
item.dc:creator: Lin Chen
Zimei Wei
Shukan Liu
Qiao Sun
Xianfeng Zhang
Bin Hu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Remote sensing change detection is constrained by the high cost of pixel-level annotations, which limits the scalability of deep models for large-area monitoring. Semi-supervised learning (SSL) can leverage unlabeled data, yet existing approaches often rely on generic natural-image augmentations and structure-agnostic representations, resulting in unreliable pseudo-labels under remote-sensing-specific appearance variations such as haze-like veiling, illumination/exposure differences, and sensor noise. In this paper, we propose PhysSANet, a physics-inspired and structure-aware SSL framework for remote sensing change detection. PhysSANet introduces a Physics-Aware Perturbation (PAP) strategy that simulates common acquisition-related variations by sampling parameters from typical ranges and adapting them using image statistics. PAP is metadata-free and does not attempt to recover physical quantities. To further improve boundary-accurate localization, PhysSANet couples PAP with a multi-head structure-aware attention module and a spatial–structural attention enhanced feature pyramid network, which explicitly model geometric cues including boundaries, regional coherence, and multi-scale spatial structure. Moreover, we design a multi-view consistency regularization scheme that integrates teacher–student consistency with appearance-plausibility-aware pseudo-label filtering and structure-aware smoothness, suppressing unreliable pseudo-labels under strong appearance variations. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets (LEVIRCD, WHU-CD, and SYSU-CD) demonstrate the effectiveness of PhysSANet across varying supervision levels. With only 5% labeled data, PhysSANet achieves 82.3% IoU on WHU-CD, 81.9% on LEVIR-CD, and 63.6% on SYSU-CD, consistently outperforming strong semi-supervised baselines. These results demonstrate that combining physics-inspired perturbations with structure-aware representation learning improves label efficiency, robustness, and boundary accuracy f... Published: 2026-04-27T19:49:30+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.783 (must_read)

Semi-supervised learning (SSL) can leverage unlabeled data, yet existing approaches often rely on generic natural-image augmentations and structure-agnostic representations, resulting in unreliable pseudo-labels under remote-sensing-specific appearance variations such as haze-like veiling, illumination/exposure differences, and sensor noise. Moreover, we design a multi-view consistency regularization scheme that integrates teacher–student consistency with appearance-plausibility-aware pseudo-label filtering and structure-aware smoothness, suppressing unreliable pseudo-labels under strong appearance variations. item.title: DIR-HSI: Degradation-Invariant Representation Learning for Hyperspectral Image Classification
item.dc:creator: Boxiang Yang
Ning Chen
Yingbo Fan
Zexi Shi
Haoyuan Zhang
Hui Liu
Run Niu
Shanjun Mao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: In recent years, Hyperspectral Image (HSI) classification has achieved remarkable progress in fine-grained surface recognition and intelligent remote sensing analysis. However, real-world degradations—such as sensor noise, compression artifacts, and atmospheric interference—often introduce severe domain shifts, leading to degraded robustness and limited generalization of existing models. To address this challenge, we propose DIR-HSI, a degradation-invariant hyperspectral classification framework designed to enhance feature stability and discriminability under multi-degradation conditions. The framework comprises two key modules: the DIR-FFE (Degradation-Invariant Representation by Fourier-domain Feature Extraction) module, which leverages Fourier-domain feature decomposition to extract discriminative and degradation-insensitive frequency features, and the DIR-CE (Degradation-Invariant Representation Contrastive Enhancement) module, which employs cross-degradation supervised contrastive learning to enforce consistency among same-class representations across different degradation conditions. Together, these components enable effective disentanglement of degradation-sensitive and stable factors, maintaining feature consistency and reinforcing class separability. The key advantage of DIR-HSI lies in its ability to jointly model degradation-invariant and discriminative representations, achieving robust and generalizable performance across complex degradation scenarios. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that DIR-HSI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, robustness, and generalization. The code will be available at https://github.com/yangboxiang1207/DIR-HSI. Published: 2026-04-27T19:49:30+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Boxiang Yang; Ning Chen; Yingbo Fan; Zexi Shi; Haoyuan Zhang; Hui Liu; Run Niu; Shanjun Mao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3687650

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

In recent years, Hyperspectral Image (HSI) classification has achieved remarkable progress in fine-grained surface recognition and intelligent remote sensing analysis. However, real-world degradations—such as sensor noise, compression artifacts, and atmospheric interference—often introduce severe domain shifts, leading to degraded robustness and limited generalization of existing models. To address this challenge, we propose DIR-HSI, a degradation-invariant hyperspectral classification framework designed to enhance feature stability and discriminability under multi-degradation conditions. The framework comprises two key modules: the DIR-FFE (Degradation-Invariant Representation by Fourier-domain Feature Extraction) module, which leverages Fourier-domain feature decomposition to extract discriminative and degradation-insensitive frequency features, and the DIR-CE (Degradation-Invariant Representation Contrastive Enhancement) module, which employs cross-degradation supervised contrastive learning to enforce consistency among same-class representations across different degradation conditions. Together, these components enable effective disentanglement of degradation-sensitive and stable factors, maintaining feature consistency and reinforcing class separability. The key advantage of DIR-HSI lies in its ability to jointly model degradation-invariant and discriminative representations, achieving robust and generalizable performance across complex degradation scenarios. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that DIR-HSI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, robustness, and generalization. The code will be available at https://github.com/yangboxiang1207/DIR-HSI.

item.title: RoundGrad: Round and round we go, optimizing quantization as we flow
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Santavas
Bram Verhoef
Antonios Gasteratos
item.prism:publicationName: Neurocomputing
item.description: Deploying deep neural networks on resource-constrained hardware demands aggressive quantization, yet the rounding scheme applied during this process is almost universally chosen for implementation convenience rather than optimized for accuracy. We introduce RoundGrad, a method that treats weight rounding as a first-order optimization problem. Starting from a pretrained model with frozen weights, RoundGrad maintains a learnable score for each weight that determines whether the value rounds up or down; these scores are updated jointly across the entire network via standard backpropagation under a regularized task-loss objective, without layer-wise reconstruction or second-order approximations. Unlike calibration-based post-training quantization methods, RoundGrad optimizes on labeled training data, placing it between traditional PTQ and full quantization-aware training. On ImageNet classification, RoundGrad achieves near-lossless 4-bit weight quantization across standard CNN architectures, using substantially fewer gradient steps than quantization-aware training (up to " role="presentation"> fewer than standard QAT schedules). Results on Pascal VOC semantic segmentation confirm that the learned rounding policy transfers to dense prediction tasks. The simplicity of the formulation and its competitive accuracy at the deployment-critical 4-bit regime make RoundGrad a practical and principled alternative to existing rounding approaches. Published: 2026-04-27T23:42:30+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.781 (must_read)

Authors: Nicholas Santavas; Bram Verhoef; Antonios Gasteratos

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133785

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

Deploying deep neural networks on resource-constrained hardware demands aggressive quantization, yet the rounding scheme applied during this process is almost universally chosen for implementation convenience rather than optimized for accuracy. We introduce RoundGrad, a method that treats weight rounding as a first-order optimization problem. Starting from a pretrained model with frozen weights, RoundGrad maintains a learnable score for each weight that determines whether the value rounds up or down; these scores are updated jointly across the entire network via standard backpropagation under a regularized task-loss objective, without layer-wise reconstruction or second-order approximations. Unlike calibration-based post-training quantization methods, RoundGrad optimizes on labeled training data, placing it between traditional PTQ and full quantization-aware training. On ImageNet classification, RoundGrad achieves near-lossless 4-bit weight quantization across standard CNN architectures, using substantially fewer gradient steps than quantization-aware training (up to " role="presentation"> fewer than standard QAT schedules). Results on Pascal VOC semantic segmentation confirm that the learned rounding policy transfers to dense prediction tasks. The simplicity of the formulation and its competitive accuracy at the deployment-critical 4-bit regime make RoundGrad a practical and principled alternative to existing rounding approaches.

item.title: Noise2Map: End-to-End Diffusion Model for Semantic Segmentation and Change Detection
item.dc:creator: Ali Shibli
Andrea Nascetti
Yifang Ban
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Semantic segmentation and change detection are two fundamental challenges in remote sensing, requiring models to capture either spatial semantics or temporal differences from satellite imagery. Existing deep learning models often struggle with temporal inconsistencies or in capturing fine-grained spatial structures, require extensive pretraining, and offer limited interpretability— especially in real-world remote sensing scenarios. Recent advances in diffusion models show that Gaussian noise can be systematically leveraged to learn expressive data representations through denoising. Motivated by this, we investigate whether the noise process in diffusion models can be effectively utilized for discriminative tasks. We propose Noise2Map, a unified diffusion-based framework that repurposes the denoising process for fast, end-to-end discriminative learning. Unlike prior work that uses diffusion only for generation or feature extraction, Noise2Map directly predicts semantic or change maps using task-specific noise schedules and timestep conditioning, avoiding the costly sampling procedures of traditional diffusion models. The model is pretrained via self-supervised denoising and fine-tuned with supervision, enabling both interpretability and robustness. Our architecture supports both tasks (SS and CD) through a shared backbone and task-specific noise schedulers. Extensive evaluations on the SpaceNet7, WHU, and xView2 buildings damaged by wildfires datasets demonstrate that Noise2Map ranks on average 1st among seven models on semantic segmentation and 1st on change detection by a cross-dataset rank metric (average F1 primary, IoU tie-break), while being 13× faster and 3× smaller than the generative diffusion baseline (DDPM-CD) due to its single-step discriminative inference. Ablation studies highlight the robustness of our model against different training noise schedulers and timestep control in the diffusion process, as well as the ability of the model to perform multi-ta... Published: 2026-04-27T19:49:30+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.779 (must_read)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3687393

Semantic segmentation and change detection are two fundamental challenges in remote sensing, requiring models to capture either spatial semantics or temporal differences from satellite imagery. Existing deep learning models often struggle with temporal inconsistencies or in capturing fine-grained spatial structures, require extensive pretraining, and offer limited interpretability— especially in real-world remote sensing scenarios. Recent advances in diffusion models show that Gaussian noise can be systematically leveraged to learn expressive data representations through denoising. Motivated by this, we investigate whether the noise process in diffusion models can be effectively utilized for discriminative tasks. We propose Noise2Map, a unified diffusion-based framework that repurposes the denoising process for fast, end-to-end discriminative learning. Unlike prior work that uses diffusion only for generation or feature extraction, Noise2Map directly predicts semantic or change maps using task-specific noise schedules and timestep conditioning, avoiding the costly sampling procedures of traditional diffusion models. The model is pretrained via self-supervised denoising and fine-tuned with supervision, enabling both interpretability and robustness. Our architecture supports both tasks (SS and CD) through a shared backbone and task-specific noise schedulers. Extensive evaluations on the SpaceNet7, WHU, and xView2 buildings damaged by wildfires datasets demonstrate that Noise2Map ranks on average 1st among seven models on semantic segmentation and 1st on change detection by a cross-dataset rank metric (average F1 primary, IoU tie-break), while being 13× faster and 3× smaller than the generative diffusion baseline (DDPM-CD) due to its single-step discriminative inference. Ablation studies highlight the robustness of our model against different training noise schedulers and timestep control in the diffusion process, as well as the ability of the model to perform multi-ta...

item.title: DiffuSAM: Diffusion-Based Prompt-Free SAM2 for Few-Shot and Source-Free Medical Image Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Tal Grossman
Noa Cahan
Lev Ayzenberg
Hayit Greenspan
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Segmentation models such as Segment Anything Model (SAM) and SAM2 achieve strong prompt-driven zero-shot performance. However, their training on natural images limits domain transfer to medical data. Consequently, accurate segmentation typically requires extensive fine-tuning and expert-designed prompts. We propose DiffuSAM, a diffusion-based adaptation of SAM2 for prompt-free medical image segmentation. Our framework synthesizes SAM2-compatible segmentation mask-like embeddings via a lightweight diffusion-prior from off-the-shelf frozen SAM2 image features. The generated embeddings are integrated into SAM2's mask decoder to produce accurate segmentations, thereby eliminating the need for user prompts. The diffusion prior is further conditioned on previously segmented slices, enforcing spatial consistency across volumes. Evaluated on the BTCV and CHAOS datasets for CT and MRI under Source-Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (SF-UDA) and Few-Shot settings, DiffuSAM achieves competitive performance with efficient training and inference. Code is available upon request from the corresponding author. Published: 2026-04-27T17:29:53+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.778 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tal Grossman; Noa Cahan; Lev Ayzenberg; Hayit Greenspan

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

Segmentation models such as Segment Anything Model (SAM) and SAM2 achieve strong prompt-driven zero-shot performance. However, their training on natural images limits domain transfer to medical data. Consequently, accurate segmentation typically requires extensive fine-tuning and expert-designed prompts. We propose DiffuSAM, a diffusion-based adaptation of SAM2 for prompt-free medical image segmentation. Our framework synthesizes SAM2-compatible segmentation mask-like embeddings via a lightweight diffusion-prior from off-the-shelf frozen SAM2 image features. The generated embeddings are integrated into SAM2's mask decoder to produce accurate segmentations, thereby eliminating the need for user prompts. The diffusion prior is further conditioned on previously segmented slices, enforcing spatial consistency across volumes. Evaluated on the BTCV and CHAOS datasets for CT and MRI under Source-Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (SF-UDA) and Few-Shot settings, DiffuSAM achieves competitive performance with efficient training and inference. Code is available upon request from the corresponding author.
